title: Graphs of Functions
objectives: Sketch graphs of functions by plotting points
Identify key features: intercepts, symmetry, increasing/decreasing intervals
Use the vertical line test to determine if a curve is a function
Recognize and graph common parent functions (linear, quadratic, cubic, square root, absolute value)
Determine whether a function is even, odd, or neither

devotional.title: Seeing What Numbers Cannot Show
devotional.content:
When God gave Moses instructions for the tabernacle, He didn't simply 
provide a list of dimensions. "See that you make them according to the 
pattern shown you on the mountain" (Exodus 25:40). There was something to 
*see*—a visual pattern that conveyed meaning beyond mere numbers.

Graphs serve a similar purpose in mathematics. A formula like 
$f(x) = x^3 - 3x$ tells us how to compute outputs, but it doesn't 
immediately reveal that the function rises, then falls, then rises again, 
creating a distinctive S-curve with two turning points. The graph shows 
us the whole story at a glance.

Human beings are visual creatures—we process images differently than 
we process words or symbols. A graph speaks to our intuition, revealing 
patterns, behaviors, and relationships that might take pages of algebra 
to describe. As you learn to read and create graphs, you're developing 
a kind of mathematical sight, a way of seeing that complements calculation.

content.text:
A graph provides a visual representation of a function, revealing behavior 
that may not be obvious from a formula alone. Where does the function 
increase? Decrease? Where does it cross the axes? Does it have any special 
symmetry? These questions are answered at a glance by a good graph.

content.title: Graph of a Function
content.content:
The **graph** of a function $f$ is the set of all 
points $(x, y)$ in the coordinate plane such that $x$ is in the domain 
of $f$ and $y = f(x)$.

In set notation: $\{(x, f(x)) : x \in \text{domain of } f\}$

content.text:
In other words, the graph consists of all points whose $y$-coordinate 
equals the function value at the $x$-coordinate. The horizontal axis 
represents the input, and the vertical axis represents the output. Every 
point on the graph tells us: "When the input is this $x$-value, the 
output is this $y$-value."

content.title: Plotting points to sketch a graph
content.problem: Sketch the graph of $f(x) = x^2 - 4$ by plotting points.
content.solution:
We create a table of values:

| $x$ | $f(x) = x^2 - 4$ | Point |
| $-3$ | $9 - 4 = 5$ | $(-3, 5)$ |
| $-2$ | $4 - 4 = 0$ | $(-2, 0)$ |
| $-1$ | $1 - 4 = -3$ | $(-1, -3)$ |
| $0$ | $0 - 4 = -4$ | $(0, -4)$ |
| $1$ | $1 - 4 = -3$ | $(1, -3)$ |
| $2$ | $4 - 4 = 0$ | $(2, 0)$ |
| $3$ | $9 - 4 = 5$ | $(3, 5)$ |

Plotting these points and connecting them with a smooth curve gives the 
parabola:

figure[H]

Graph of $f(x) = x^2 - 4$, a parabola opening upward with vertex at $(0, -4)$ and $x$-intercepts at $x = \pm 2$. Plotted points include $(-3, 5)$, $(-2, 0)$, $(-1, -3)$, $(0, -4)$, $(1, -3)$, $(2, 0)$, and $(3, 5)$.

figure

figures.caption: Graph of $f(x) = x^2 - 4$, a parabola opening upward with vertex at $(0, -4)$ and $x$-intercepts at $x = \pm 2$. Plotted points include $(-3, 5)$, $(-2, 0)$, $(-1, -3)$, $(0, -4)$, $(1, -3)$, $(2, 0)$, and $(3, 5)$.
figures.alt: Graph of $f(x) = x^2 - 4$, a parabola opening upward with vertex at $(0, -4)$ and $x$-intercepts at $x = \pm 2$. Plotted points include $(-3, 5)$, $(-2, 0)$, $(-1, -3)$, $(0, -4)$, $(1, -3)$, $(2, 0)$, and $(3, 5)$.

content.text:
Not every curve in the plane is the graph of a function. The defining 
property of a function—exactly one output for each input—has a 
geometric interpretation that gives us a quick visual test.

content.title: The Vertical Line Test
content.content:
A curve in the coordinate plane is the graph of a function if and only 
if every vertical line intersects the curve at most once.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
Why does this work? A vertical line through $x = a$ consists of all points 
with $x$-coordinate $a$. If this line crosses the curve twice, say at 
$(a, b)$ and $(a, c)$ with $b \neq c$, then the input $a$ would produce 
two different outputs $b$ and $c$. That contradicts the definition of a 
function.

content.title: Applying the vertical line test
content.problem:
Determine whether each curve represents a function.

figure[H]

Vertical line test examples. (a) Parabola: passes the test (each vertical line crosses at most once). (b) Circle: fails the test (the dashed vertical line crosses twice, marked with dots). (c) S-curve: passes the test.

figure
content.solution:
1. **Yes, this is a function.** Every vertical line crosses 
 the parabola at most once. The dashed line shown intersects exactly once.

2. **No, this is not a function.** Many vertical lines cross 
 the circle twice. The dashed line at $x = 0.8$ intersects at two points 
 (marked), meaning input $0.8$ would have two outputs.

3. **Yes, this is a function.** Every vertical line crosses 
 the S-curve at most once.

figures.caption: Vertical line test examples. (a) Parabola: passes the test (each vertical line crosses at most once). (b) Circle: fails the test (the dashed vertical line crosses twice, marked with dots). (c) S-curve: passes the test.
figures.alt: Vertical line test examples. (a) Parabola: passes the test (each vertical line crosses at most once). (b) Circle: fails the test (the dashed vertical line crosses twice, marked with dots). (c) S-curve: passes the test.

content.title: Vertical vs. Horizontal Line Tests
content.content:
The *vertical* line test determines whether a curve is a function. 
The *horizontal* line test (which we'll see in Section [sec:inverse-functions]) 
determines whether a function is one-to-one. Don't confuse them!

content.text: Given the graph of $f$, we can determine function values visually:

items: Locate $x = a$ on the horizontal axis
Draw a vertical line up (or down) to the graph
Read the $y$-coordinate of the intersection point
Locate $y = b$ on the vertical axis
Draw a horizontal line to find all intersections with the graph
Read the $x$-coordinates of all intersection points

content.title: Reading values from a graph
content.problem:
Use the graph of $f$ shown below to estimate the following.

figure[H]

Graph of a cubic function for reading function values. The curve passes through approximately $(1, 2.3)$, $(2, 2)$, $(3, 1.7)$, and $(4, 3)$.

figure

1. $f(1)$

2. $f(3)$

3. All values of $x$ where $f(x) = 2$

4. All values of $x$ where $f(x) = 3$
content.solution:
1. Looking at $x = 1$ and reading the $y$-value: $f(1) \approx 2.3$

2. Looking at $x = 3$ and reading the $y$-value: $f(3) \approx 1.7$

3. Drawing a horizontal line at $y = 2$, we find intersections at 
 approximately $x \approx 0.5$, $x = 2$, and $x \approx 3.5$.

4. Drawing a horizontal line at $y = 3$, we find one intersection 
 at approximately $x \approx 4$.

figures.caption: Graph of a cubic function for reading function values. The curve passes through approximately $(1, 2.3)$, $(2, 2)$, $(3, 1.7)$, and $(4, 3)$.
figures.alt: Graph of a cubic function for reading function values. The curve passes through approximately $(1, 2.3)$, $(2, 2)$, $(3, 1.7)$, and $(4, 3)$.

content.title: Intercepts
content.content:
The **$y$-intercept** of a graph is 
the point where it crosses the $y$-axis, if such a point exists. It has 
coordinates $(0, f(0))$.

The **$x$-intercepts** (also called 
**zeros** or **roots**) are the 
points where the graph crosses the $x$-axis. They have coordinates $(a, 0)$ 
where $f(a) = 0$.

content.title: Finding intercepts algebraically
content.problem: Find the intercepts of $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3$.
content.solution:
**$y$-intercept:** Set $x = 0$:
$$
 f(0) = 0^2 - 4(0) + 3 = 3
$$
The $y$-intercept is $(0, 3)$.

**$x$-intercepts:** Solve $f(x) = 0$:
$$
 x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0 \implies (x - 1)(x - 3) = 0 \implies x = 1 \text{ or } x = 3
$$
The $x$-intercepts are $(1, 0)$ and $(3, 0)$.

content.title: Finding intercepts of a rational function
content.problem: Find the intercepts of $g(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 4}{x + 1}$.
content.solution:
**$y$-intercept:** Set $x = 0$:
$$
 g(0) = \frac{0 - 4}{0 + 1} = \frac{-4}{1} = -4
$$
The $y$-intercept is $(0, -4)$.

**$x$-intercepts:** A fraction equals zero when its numerator is zero 
(and denominator is nonzero):
$$
 x^2 - 4 = 0 \implies (x-2)(x+2) = 0 \implies x = 2 \text{ or } x = -2
$$
Check that neither makes the denominator zero: $2 + 1 = 3 \neq 0$ and 
$-2 + 1 = -1 \neq 0$. Good!

The $x$-intercepts are $(2, 0)$ and $(-2, 0)$.

content.title: Increasing and Decreasing
content.content:
A function $f$ is **increasing** on an 
interval $I$ if for all $a, b \in I$:
$$
 a < b \implies f(a) < f(b)
$$

A function $f$ is **decreasing** on an 
interval $I$ if for all $a, b \in I$:
$$
 a < b \implies f(a) > f(b)
$$

content.text:
In plain language: a function is increasing where its graph rises as you 
move from left to right, and decreasing where its graph falls. When 
describing where a function increases or decreases, we always state 
intervals of $x$-values (input intervals), not $y$-values.

content.title: Identifying intervals of increase and decrease
content.problem:
From the graph below, identify the intervals on which $f$ is increasing 
and decreasing.

figure[H]

Graph showing intervals of increase and decrease. The function increases on $(-\infty, -1)$, decreases on $(-1, 1)$, and increases on $(1, \infty)$. Local maximum at $(-1, 0.6)$ and local minimum at $(1, -0.6)$ are marked.

figure
content.solution:
Reading from left to right:

- The function **increases** from $x = -\infty$ to $x = -1$ 
 (the graph rises)

- The function **decreases** from $x = -1$ to $x = 1$ 
 (the graph falls)

- The function **increases** from $x = 1$ to $x = \infty$ 
 (the graph rises again)

In interval notation:

- Increasing on $(-\infty, -1)$ and $(1, \infty)$

- Decreasing on $(-1, 1)$

figures.caption: Graph showing intervals of increase and decrease. The function increases on $(-\infty, -1)$, decreases on $(-1, 1)$, and increases on $(1, \infty)$. Local maximum at $(-1, 0.6)$ and local minimum at $(1, -0.6)$ are marked.
figures.alt: Graph showing intervals of increase and decrease. The function increases on $(-\infty, -1)$, decreases on $(-1, 1)$, and increases on $(1, \infty)$. Local maximum at $(-1, 0.6)$ and local minimum at $(1, -0.6)$ are marked.

content.title: Algebraic analysis of increasing/decreasing
content.problem: Show that $f(x) = x^3$ is increasing on $(-\infty, \infty)$.
content.solution:
We need to show that if $a < b$, then $f(a) < f(b)$, i.e., $a^3 < b^3$.

Consider $b^3 - a^3$. We can factor this as:
$$
 b^3 - a^3 = (b - a)(b^2 + ab + a^2)
$$

Since $a < b$, we have $b - a > 0$.

For the second factor, complete the square:
$$
 b^2 + ab + a^2 = \left(b + \frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{3a^2}{4} > 0
$$
(This is always positive because it's a sum of squares.)

Since both factors are positive, $b^3 - a^3 > 0$, which means $a^3 < b^3$.

Therefore, $f(x) = x^3$ is increasing on its entire domain.

content.title: Local Maximum and Minimum
content.content:
A function $f$ has a **local maximum** at $c$ 
if $f(c) \ge f(x)$ for all $x$ in some open interval containing $c$.

A function $f$ has a **local minimum** at $c$ 
if $f(c) \le f(x)$ for all $x$ in some open interval containing $c$.

Collectively, local maxima and minima are called **local extrema**.

content.text:
Graphically, local maxima appear as "peaks" and local minima as 
"valleys." At these points, the function transitions from increasing 
to decreasing (maximum) or from decreasing to increasing (minimum).

content.title: Even and Odd Functions
content.content:
A function $f$ is **even** if $f(-x) = f(x)$ 
for all $x$ in its domain. The graph of an even function is symmetric 
about the $y$-axis.

A function $f$ is **odd** if $f(-x) = -f(x)$ 
for all $x$ in its domain. The graph of an odd function is symmetric 
about the origin.

content.text:
Why these names? Even powers of $x$ (like $x^2$, $x^4$, $x^6$) satisfy 
$(-x)^n = x^n$, so they are even functions. Odd powers (like $x$, $x^3$, 
$x^5$) satisfy $(-x)^n = -x^n$, so they are odd functions.

content.caption: Even vs.\ odd functions. Left: $f(x) = x^2$ is even, symmetric about the $y$-axis—points $(-1.5, 2.25)$ and $(1.5, 2.25)$ have the same $y$-value. Right: $f(x) = x^3$ is odd, symmetric about the origin—points $(-1.2, -1.73)$ and $(1.2, 1.73)$ are reflections through the origin.
content.alt: Even vs.\ odd functions. Left: $f(x) = x^2$ is even, symmetric about the $y$-axis—points $(-1.5, 2.25)$ and $(1.5, 2.25)$ have the same $y$-value. Right: $f(x) = x^3$ is odd, symmetric about the origin—points $(-1.2, -1.73)$ and $(1.2, 1.73)$ are reflections through the origin.

content.title: Determining even or odd
content.problem:
Determine whether each function is even, odd, or neither.

1. $f(x) = x^4 - 3x^2 + 1$

2. $g(x) = x^3 - x$

3. $h(x) = x^2 + x$

4. $p(x) = \dfrac{x}{x^2 + 1}$
content.solution:
The method: compute $f(-x)$ and compare it to $f(x)$ and $-f(x)$.

1. Compute $f(-x)$:
 $$
 f(-x) = (-x)^4 - 3(-x)^2 + 1 = x^4 - 3x^2 + 1 = f(x)
 $$
 Since $f(-x) = f(x)$, the function is **even**.

2. Compute $g(-x)$:
 $$
 g(-x) = (-x)^3 - (-x) = -x^3 + x = -(x^3 - x) = -g(x)
 $$
 Since $g(-x) = -g(x)$, the function is **odd**.

3. Compute $h(-x)$:
 $$
 h(-x) = (-x)^2 + (-x) = x^2 - x
 $$
 Compare: $h(-x) = x^2 - x$ but $h(x) = x^2 + x$, so $h(-x) \neq h(x)$.
 
 Also: $-h(x) = -x^2 - x \neq h(-x)$.
 
 The function is **neither even nor odd**.

4. Compute $p(-x)$:
 $$
 p(-x) = \frac{-x}{(-x)^2 + 1} = \frac{-x}{x^2 + 1} = -\frac{x}{x^2 + 1} = -p(x)
 $$
 Since $p(-x) = -p(x)$, the function is **odd**.

content.title: Properties of Even and Odd Functions
content.content:
1. The sum of two even functions is even.

2. The sum of two odd functions is odd.

3. The product of two even functions is even.

4. The product of two odd functions is even.

5. The product of an even and an odd function is odd.

6. The only function that is both even and odd is $f(x) = 0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
Certain functions appear so frequently that you should know their shapes 
by heart. These "building blocks" will be transformed in various ways 
throughout this course.

content.caption: Linear and constant functions. Left: constant function $f(x) = c$, a horizontal line. Right: identity function $f(x) = x$, a diagonal line through the origin with slope 1.
content.alt: Linear and constant functions. Left: constant function $f(x) = c$, a horizontal line. Right: identity function $f(x) = x$, a diagonal line through the origin with slope 1.

content.caption: Power functions. Left: $f(x) = x^2$, a parabola (even function). Center: $f(x) = x^3$, an S-shaped curve (odd function). Right: $f(x) = x^4$, similar to $x^2$ but flatter near the origin and steeper away from it.
content.alt: Power functions. Left: $f(x) = x^2$, a parabola (even function). Center: $f(x) = x^3$, an S-shaped curve (odd function). Right: $f(x) = x^4$, similar to $x^2$ but flatter near the origin and steeper away from it.

content.caption: Root functions. Left: $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$, defined only for $x \geq 0$, starts at the origin and increases slowly. Right: $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$, defined for all real $x$, an odd function passing through the origin.
content.alt: Root functions. Left: $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$, defined only for $x \geq 0$, starts at the origin and increases slowly. Right: $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$, defined for all real $x$, an odd function passing through the origin.

content.caption: Reciprocal functions with vertical asymptotes at $x = 0$ (dashed line). Left: $f(x) = 1/x$, an odd function with two branches in opposite quadrants. Right: $f(x) = 1/x^2$, an even function with both branches above the $x$-axis.
content.alt: Reciprocal functions with vertical asymptotes at $x = 0$ (dashed line). Left: $f(x) = 1/x$, an odd function with two branches in opposite quadrants. Right: $f(x) = 1/x^2$, an even function with both branches above the $x$-axis.

content.caption: Absolute value function $f(x) = |x|$, a V-shaped graph with vertex at the origin. The function equals $x$ for $x \geq 0$ and $-x$ for $x < 0$. It is an even function.
content.alt: Absolute value function $f(x) = |x|$, a V-shaped graph with vertex at the origin. The function equals $x$ for $x \geq 0$ and $-x$ for $x < 0$. It is an even function.

content.text:
The **greatest integer function** 
(also called the **floor function**), written 
$\lfloor x \rfloor$, returns the greatest integer less than or equal to $x$.

content.caption:
The greatest integer (floor) function $f(x) = \lfloor x \rfloor$. 
Each "step" spans one unit, with a closed dot at the left endpoint and an 
open dot at the right. For example, $\lfloor 2.7 \rfloor = 2$ and 
$\lfloor -1.3 \rfloor = -2$.
content.alt:
The greatest integer (floor) function $f(x) = \lfloor x \rfloor$. 
Each "step" spans one unit, with a closed dot at the left endpoint and an 
open dot at the right. For example, $\lfloor 2.7 \rfloor = 2$ and 
$\lfloor -1.3 \rfloor = -2$.

content.text:
$\lceil x \rceil$, 
returns the *smallest* integer greater than or equal to $x$.}

content.title: Graphs of Functions
content.content:
- The graph of $f$ consists of all points $(x, f(x))$.

- **Vertical line test:** A curve is a function iff every 
 vertical line crosses it at most once.

- The **$y$-intercept** is $(0, f(0))$; **$x$-intercepts** 
 are where $f(x) = 0$.

- A function is **increasing** where its graph rises left-to-right; 
 **decreasing** where it falls.

- **Even functions** satisfy $f(-x) = f(x)$ ($y$-axis symmetry).

- **Odd functions** satisfy $f(-x) = -f(x)$ (origin symmetry).

content.problem:
Use the vertical line test to determine whether each curve is the graph 
of a function.

1. A horizontal line

2. A vertical line

3. A parabola opening to the right (like $x = y^2$)

4. The graph of $x^2 + y^2 = 4$

5. The upper half of a circle

Explain in your own words why a circle cannot be the graph of a function, 
but a semicircle can.

If a function has two $x$-intercepts, what can you conclude about the 
function? Can a function have no $x$-intercepts? Give an example.

True or False? Justify your answer.

1. A function can have more than one $y$-intercept.

2. A function can have more than one $x$-intercept.

3. If a function is even, its graph is symmetric about the $y$-axis.

4. If a function is odd, its graph passes through the origin.

5. A function cannot be both even and odd.

True or False? Justify your answer.

1. If $f$ is increasing on $(a, b)$ and on $(b, c)$, then $f$ is 
 increasing on $(a, c)$.

2. The sum of two even functions is even.

3. The sum of two odd functions is odd.

4. The product of an even function and an odd function is odd.

%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
% 3. VERTICAL LINE TEST
%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

**Using the Vertical Line Test**

Sketch a graph that:

1. Is a function

2. Is not a function

3. Passes through $(0, 2)$ and $(3, 0)$ and is a function

4. Passes through $(2, 1)$ and $(2, 3)$ and is not a function

Determine whether each equation defines $y$ as a function of $x$.

1. $y = x^2 - 4$

2. $x = y^2 - 4$

3. $x^2 + y^2 = 25$

4. $y = \sqrt{25 - x^2}$

5. $|y| = x$

6. $y = |x|$

Sketch the graph of each function by plotting points.

1. $f(x) = 2x - 3$

2. $g(x) = -x + 4$

3. $h(x) = x^2 - 1$

4. $k(x) = -x^2 + 2$

Sketch the graph of each function by plotting points.

1. $f(x) = x^3$

2. $g(x) = \sqrt{x}$

3. $h(x) = |x - 1|$

4. $k(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ for $x > 0$

Find the $x$- and $y$-intercepts of each function.

1. $f(x) = 2x - 8$

2. $g(x) = x^2 - 9$

3. $h(x) = x^2 + 4x + 3$

4. $k(x) = x^3 - 4x$

Find the $x$- and $y$-intercepts of each function.

1. $f(x) = \sqrt{x - 4}$

2. $g(x) = |x - 3| - 2$

3. $h(x) = \dfrac{x - 2}{x + 1}$

4. $k(x) = x^2 - 5x + 6$

Find the $x$- and $y$-intercepts of each function.

1. $f(x) = (x + 1)(x - 2)(x - 4)$

2. $g(x) = x^4 - 5x^2 + 4$

3. $h(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 4}{x - 3}$

4. $k(x) = |2x + 1| - 3$

Find all intercepts of each function.

1. $f(x) = x^2 - 5x + 6$

2. $g(x) = x^3 - 4x$

3. $h(x) = \dfrac{x - 2}{x + 3}$

4. $p(x) = \sqrt{x - 4}$

5. $q(x) = x^2 - 2x - 8$

6. $r(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 9}{x - 2}$

Find the $x$- and $y$-intercepts of each function.

1. $f(x) = 2x^2 - 7x + 3$

2. $g(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 6x$

3. $h(x) = |x - 5| - 4$

4. $k(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 16}{x + 2}$

Find the zeros of each function algebraically.

1. $f(x) = 3x + 12$

2. $g(x) = 5 - 2x$

3. $h(x) = x^2 - 16$

4. $k(x) = x^2 + 5x + 6$

Find the zeros of each function algebraically.

1. $f(x) = 2x^2 - 7x - 15$

2. $g(x) = x^2 - 10x + 25$

3. $h(x) = 6x^2 + x - 2$

4. $k(x) = x^3 - 4x$

Find the zeros of each function algebraically.

1. $f(x) = \dfrac{x + 5}{x - 2}$

2. $g(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 9}{x + 1}$

3. $h(x) = \dfrac{2x - 6}{x^2 - 4}$

4. $k(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - x - 12}{x^2 + 2x}$

Find the zeros of each function algebraically.

1. $f(x) = \sqrt{2x - 8}$

2. $g(x) = \sqrt{x + 3} - 2$

3. $h(x) = \sqrt{5 - x} - 3$

4. $k(x) = \sqrt{x - 1} - \sqrt{3 - x}$

Find the zeros of each function algebraically.

1. $f(x) = x^3 - 9x$

2. $g(x) = x^4 - 5x^2 + 4$

3. $h(x) = x^3 - 2x^2 - 4x + 8$

4. $k(x) = x^4 - 13x^2 + 36$

Determine the intervals on which each function is increasing and decreasing.

1. $f(x) = 3x + 1$

2. $g(x) = -2x + 5$

3. $h(x) = x^2$

4. $k(x) = -x^2 + 4$

Determine the intervals on which each function is increasing and decreasing.

1. $f(x) = x^2 - 4x$

2. $g(x) = x^2 + 6x + 5$

3. $h(x) = |x - 2|$

4. $k(x) = \sqrt{x}$

Determine the intervals on which each function is increasing and decreasing.

1. $f(x) = x^3$

2. $g(x) = x^3 - 3x$

3. $h(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ for $x > 0$

4. $k(x) = |x + 1| + |x - 1|$

Determine the intervals on which each function is increasing and decreasing.

1. $f(x) = -x^2 + 6x - 5$

2. $g(x) = x^3 - 12x$

3. $h(x) = |x + 3| - 2$

4. $k(x) = \sqrt{x - 1}$

Identify the relative maxima and minima of each function from its graph. 
Estimate the coordinates of each extreme point.

1. $f(x) = x^3 - 3x$

2. $g(x) = x^4 - 2x^2$

3. $h(x) = -x^3 + 3x^2 - 2$

4. $k(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 4x^2$

Use a graphing utility to graph each function. Approximate 
(to two decimal places) any relative minima or maxima.

1. $f(x) = x^3 - 3x + 1$

2. $g(x) = x^4 - 4x^2 + 2$

3. $h(x) = -x^4 + 3x^2 - 1$

4. $k(x) = x^5 - 5x^3 + 4x$

Use a graphing utility to graph each function. Approximate 
(to two decimal places) any relative minima or maxima.

1. $f(x) = x\sqrt{4 - x^2}$

2. $g(x) = \dfrac{x}{x^2 + 1}$

3. $h(x) = x^2 e^{-x}$ for $x \geq 0$

4. $k(x) = x^{2/3}(x - 4)$

For each function, determine whether there is a relative maximum, 
relative minimum, both, or neither. Explain your reasoning without 
using a graphing utility.

1. $f(x) = 3x + 7$

2. $g(x) = x^2 - 6x + 5$

3. $h(x) = -x^2 + 4x - 1$

4. $k(x) = |x - 3|$

5. $p(x) = x^3$

6. $q(x) = \sqrt{x}$

Determine algebraically whether each function is even, odd, or neither.

1. $f(x) = x^4 - x^2$

2. $g(x) = x^3 - x$

3. $h(x) = x^3 + x^2$

4. $k(x) = x^4 + 1$

Determine algebraically whether each function is even, odd, or neither.

1. $f(x) = |x|$

2. $g(x) = x|x|$

3. $h(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$

4. $k(x) = \sqrt{x^2 + 1}$

Determine algebraically whether each function is even, odd, or neither.

1. $f(x) = \dfrac{x}{x^2 + 1}$

2. $g(x) = \dfrac{x^2}{x^2 - 1}$

3. $h(x) = x + \dfrac{1}{x}$

4. $k(x) = \dfrac{|x|}{x}$ for $x \neq 0$

Determine algebraically whether each function is even, odd, or neither.

1. $f(x) = 2x^5 - 3x^3 + x$

2. $g(x) = x^6 - 2x^4 + x^2$

3. $h(x) = x^3 + 2x^2 - x$

4. $k(x) = \dfrac{x^3 + x}{x^4 + 1}$

Determine whether each function is even, odd, or neither.

1. $f(x) = x^5 - x^3$

2. $g(x) = x^4 + x^3$

3. $h(x) = \dfrac{x}{x^2 + 1}$

4. $p(x) = |x| + x^2$

5. $q(x) = x^3 + 1$

6. $r(x) = \dfrac{1}{x^3}$

Use the graph of $f$ to answer each question. (Graph provided by instructor.)

1. What is $f(0)$?

2. What is the domain of $f$?

3. What is the range of $f$?

4. On what intervals is $f$ increasing?

5. On what intervals is $f$ decreasing?

6. What are the zeros of $f$?

For each function graphed, determine:

1. The domain and range

2. The $x$- and $y$-intercepts

3. The intervals where $f$ is increasing and decreasing

4. Whether $f$ is even, odd, or neither

Given the following information about a function $f$, sketch a possible graph:

- Domain: $[-4, 4]$

- Range: $[-2, 3]$

- $f(0) = 1$

- $f(-2) = f(2) = 0$

- $f$ is increasing on $(-4, 0)$

- $f$ is decreasing on $(0, 4)$

Sketch a graph of a function that satisfies all of the following:

1. Domain: $[-3, 5]$, Range: $[-2, 4]$

2. $f(0) = 1$ and $f(2) = -2$

3. Increasing on $(-3, 0)$ and $(3, 5)$

4. Decreasing on $(0, 3)$

Sketch a graph of a function that is:

1. Increasing on $(-\infty, 1)$ and decreasing on $(1, \infty)$

2. Odd and increasing on its entire domain

3. Even with exactly two $x$-intercepts

4. Neither increasing nor decreasing on any interval

The price $P$ of a stock over a 10-hour trading day is modeled by 
$P(t) = -0.1t^2 + t + 50$ for $0 \leq t \leq 10$.

1. When is the stock price increasing? Decreasing?

2. At what time is the price highest?

3. What are the opening and closing prices?
content.label: Stock Price

content.problem:
A city's population $P$ (in thousands) over 20 years is modeled by 
$P(t) = 50 + 2t - 0.05t^2$ for $0 \leq t \leq 20$.

1. When is the population increasing?

2. When does the population reach its maximum?

3. What is the maximum population?
content.label: Population

content.problem:
A ball is thrown upward with height $h(t) = -16t^2 + 64t + 5$ feet.

1. On what time interval is the ball rising?

2. When does it reach maximum height?

3. What is the maximum height?
content.label: Projectile

content.problem:
The fuel efficiency $E$ (in miles per gallon) of a car traveling at 
speed $v$ (in mph) is modeled by $E(v) = -0.02v^2 + 2v + 10$ for 
$20 \leq v \leq 70$.

1. At what speed is fuel efficiency maximized?

2. What is the maximum fuel efficiency?

3. On what interval is efficiency increasing?
content.label: Fuel Efficiency

content.problem:
A company's revenue $R$ (in thousands of dollars) based on advertising 
spending $x$ (in thousands) is $R(x) = -2x^2 + 40x + 100$ for $0 \leq x \leq 15$.

1. When is revenue increasing as advertising increases?

2. What advertising budget maximizes revenue?

3. What is the maximum revenue?
content.label: Revenue

content.problem:
The temperature $T$ (in $^\circ$F) during a 12-hour period is modeled by 
$T(t) = -0.5t^2 + 6t + 50$ for $0 \leq t \leq 12$.

1. When is the temperature increasing?

2. At what time is the temperature highest?

3. What are the temperatures at the beginning and end of the period?
content.label: Temperature

content.problem:
The population $P$ of a bacteria culture after $t$ hours is modeled by 
$P(t) = 100t - t^2$ for $0 \leq t \leq 100$.

1. When is the population increasing?

2. When does the population reach its maximum?

3. What is the maximum population?

4. When does the population return to zero?
content.label: Bacteria Growth

content.problem:
Use a graphing utility to graph each function. Then 
determine the intervals of increase and decrease.

1. $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x$

2. $g(x) = x^4 - 4x^3$

3. $h(x) = x\sqrt{4 - x^2}$

4. $k(x) = \dfrac{x^2}{x^2 + 1}$

Use a graphing utility to determine whether each function 
is even, odd, or neither.

1. $f(x) = x^2\cos x$

2. $g(x) = x\sin x$

3. $h(x) = x^2 + \sin x$

4. $k(x) = e^{-x^2}$

%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
% 13. ERROR ANALYSIS
%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

**Error Analysis**

Describe and correct the error.

Determining if $f(x) = x^2 - 4$ is even:
quote
"$f(-x) = (-x)^2 - 4 = -x^2 - 4$, which is neither $f(x)$ nor $-f(x)$, 
so $f$ is neither even nor odd." 
quote

Describe and correct the error.

Finding when $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3$ is increasing:
quote
"Since $f(0) = 3$ and $f(4) = 3$, and $f(2) = -1 < 3$, the function is 
decreasing on $(0, 2)$ and increasing on $(2, 4)$." 
quote

%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
% 14. CHALLENGE PROBLEMS
%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

**Challenge Problems**

Prove that the only function that is both even and odd is 
$f(x) = 0$.
If $f$ is an odd function and $f(3) = 7$, find $f(-3) + f(3)$.

Show that any function $f$ whose domain is symmetric about zero can be 
written as the sum of an even function and an odd function. Specifically, 
verify that:
$$
 f(x) = \underbrace{\frac{f(x) + f(-x)}{2}}_{\text{even}} + 
 \underbrace{\frac{f(x) - f(-x)}{2}}_{\text{odd}}
$$

Prove that if $f$ and $g$ are both increasing functions, 
then $f + g$ is also increasing.

Prove that if $f$ is an even function and $g$ is an odd 
function, then $fg$ is an odd function.

A function is called *periodic* with period $p$ if $f(x + p) = f(x)$ 
for all $x$. If $f$ is both even and periodic with period $p$, show that 
$f$ is also symmetric about the vertical line $x = p/2$.

Prove that if $f$ is increasing on an interval $I$ and $g$ is increasing 
on an interval $J$ where the range of $f$ is contained in $J$, then 
$g \circ f$ is increasing on $I$.
Prove that if $f$ is an odd function and $f(0)$ is defined, then $f(0) = 0$.

marginNotes.text:
The heavens declare the glory of God; the 
skies proclaim the work of his hands.

There is a time for everything... a 
time to weep and a time to laugh, a time to mourn and a time to dance.
